message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  None of the available evaluations of the inhaled air carbon dioxide (CO
                  <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                  ) concentration, while wearing face masks, used professional, real-time capnography with water-removal tubing. We measured the end-tidal CO
                  <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                  using professional side-stream capnography, with water-removing tubing (Rad-97™ capnograph), at rest, (1) without masks, (2) wearing a surgical mask, and (3) wearing a FFP2 respirator, in 102 healthy volunteers aged 10-90 years, from the general population of Ferrara province, Italy. The inhaled air CO
                  <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                  concentration was then computed as: ((mask volume × end-tidal CO
                  <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                  ) + ((tidal volume - mask volume) × ambient air CO
                  <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                  )) / tidal volume).
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  The mean CO
                  <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                  concentration was 4965±1047 ppm with surgical masks, and 9396±2254 ppm with FFP2 respirators. The proportion of the sample showing a CO
                  <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                  concentration higher than the 5000 ppm acceptable exposure threshold recommended for workers was 40.2% while wearing surgical masks, 99.0% while wearing FFP2 respirators. The mean blood oxygen saturation remained >96%, and the mean end-tidal CO
                  <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                  <33 mmHg. Adjusting for age, gender, BMI, and smoking, the inhaled air CO
                  <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                  concentration significantly increased with increasing respiratory rate (with a mean of 10,143±2782 ppm among the participants taking 18 or more breaths per minute, while wearing FFP2 respirators), and was higher among the minors, who showed a mean CO
                  <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                  concentration of 12,847±2898 ppm, while wearing FFP2 respirators. If these results will be confirmed, the current guidelines on mask-wearing could be updated to integrate recommendations for slow breathing and a more targeted use when contagion risk is low.
                </jats:p>
title:
Inhaled CO
                  <sub>2</sub>
                  concentration while wearing face masks: a pilot study using capnography
